content:
{
  "title": "Dynamic Error-Driven Prompting for Enhanced Code Generation in LLMs",
  "problem": "Current prompting strategies for code generation with large language models (LLMs) rely on static instructions or exemplars, leading to persistent mistakes in logic, syntax, or API use. Improving LLM code generation requires prompts that adaptively address specific errors as they arise.",
  "motivation": "As LLMs increasingly automate code writing, their susceptibility to subtle, repeated bugs limits adoption in critical software engineering workflows. Traditional static prompting or few-shot exemplars plateau in performance, even as model scale increases. Unlocking further gains demands prompt methods that interactively diagnose and correct failures.",
  "method": "We propose Dynamic Error-Driven Prompting (DEDP), a prompting pipeline where the model's own outputs are iteratively analyzed using lightweight static analyzers, interpreters, and test cases. Detected errors trigger targeted 'correction hints'—mini-prompts, automatically appended to the next input, that explain the specific mistake (e.g., 'The last output did not close the file handle. Please ensure all opened files are closed.') The LLM thus receives real-time, context-specific feedback, enabling it to correct itself over multiple rounds. The correction hints are generated and inserted with minimal human intervention.",
  "experiment_plan": "We benchmark DEDP on HumanEval, MBPP, and CodeContests, comparing against zero-shot, few-shot, and chain-of-thought prompting. Standard metrics include pass@k, code correctness, and syntactic/semantic error rate. Ablations test the effect of different error detectors and hint granularities."
}
